detail: Tagged A3.T8 (apostrophe/possessive) but options ('paintings were' / 'paintings,' / 'paintings, were' / 'paintings had been') vary in punctuation and finite-vs-nonfinite verb form, not in apostrophe placement. This tests A1.T2 (finite vs non-finite form) — main clause already has 'had been...overlooked,' so the blank must leave 'celebrated' as a non-finite participle inside a supplementary element offset by commas.

Tagged A2.T3 (subject-verb agreement) but the four options ('was' / 'is' / 'has been' / 'will be') all share singular number and differ only in tense. This is A1.T1 (tense selection from time cues) — answered correctly via the 1312-1337 date anchor, not via number agreement.

Tagged A2.T3 (subject-verb agreement) but options ('broadcasting' / 'having broadcast' / 'to broadcast' / 'broadcast to') vary in finite vs non-finite verb form, not in number. This is A1.T2 (finite vs non-finite form) — the main clause lacks a finite verb, so only the finite 'broadcast to' fits.

Stem has no interrupting modifier between subject 'demonstration' and verb — the blank sits inside a 'that' relative clause whose subject is 'that' (=demonstration). This is T3-like (or arguably A1, since options mix tense AND number). Answer 'advances' is correct.
Options vary in finite/non-finite form (perfect infinitive, participle, finite past, to-infinitive), not in tense. This matches T2 (Finite vs non-finite form), not T1 (Tense selection).

Clone tests punctuation (comma pairing with dash to offset a supplementary element), not verb form/finiteness. The four options vary in whether a verb ('is'/'has been') is included and whether a comma is present — the correct answer (C) is selected on punctuation grounds, not on finite vs non-finite distinction. Belongs to a punctuation aspect, not A1.T2.

Tagged as T5 inverted-order in template example_ids, but stem is standard SVO with interrupting participial phrase, not inversion. However templateId says T4 (interrupting modifier), which matches the stem structure. Verdict: ok.

Tests punctuation between subject and verb (no-punctuation vs comma/dash/colon), which is a Boundaries/Punctuation skill, not Form/Structure/Sense. Template has no 'punctuation' type under A4 (only T9 dangling modifier). Options differ in punctuation marks rather than verb/pronoun/modifier form.

Past-perfect required by 'two decades earlier' sequencing with another past event matches the medium lever ('two competing time frames, past-perfect vs simple-past discrimination'), not easy. Answer 'had designed' is correct, but item belongs at medium.
